title: Soldering a copper end-feed joint
subtitle: Clean, flux, heat the fitting, feed the solder
excerpt: How to make a watertight soldered joint on 15 mm and 22 mm copper pipe using end-feed fittings: cleaning the pipe and fitting, applying flux, heating the assembly to the correct temperature, and feeding lead-free solder until the joint fills.
sourceNotes: Standard UK copper plumbing technique. CIPHE (Chartered Institute of Plumbing and Heating Engineering) technical guidance. Lead-free solder requirement from The Water Fittings Regulations 1999. MAPP gas torch technique from Yorkshire Fittings technical data sheets.

content.text: An
end-feed joint

on copper pipe is made by capillary action: molten solder is drawn by surface tension into the narrow gap between the pipe and the fitting. The joint is watertight because the solder fills the gap completely. The prerequisite is bright, clean copper at every contact surface; solder will not bond to tarnished or dirty metal regardless of how much flux is applied.
Safety

Keep a heat-proof mat or soldering pad behind the fitting when working near timber, cables, or joists. MAPP gas burns at approximately 2000°C and will ignite wood instantly. Keep a damp cloth close. Ensure the pipe run is fully drained and dried; water in the pipe turns to steam and blows the molten solder out of the joint.
Preparation
Step 1, cut the pipe square.

Use the pipe cutter: rotate it around the pipe, tightening a quarter turn each pass. The cut must be exactly square; an angled cut leaves a gap on one side of the fitting and a poor joint. Deburr the cut end inside and outside with the reamer on the cutter or a fine file.
Step 2, clean the pipe end and fitting socket.

Wrap a strip of emery cloth around the pipe end and polish it to bright copper for 25 to 30 mm from the cut. Polish the inside of the fitting socket with the emery cloth wrapped around a finger. The metal must be bright and free of tarnish, oil, or fingermarks.
Step 3, apply flux.
Apply a thin coat of
water-soluble flux

to the pipe end and inside the fitting socket. Push the fitting home and twist once to spread the flux. Work quickly; flux left in contact with copper for more than a few minutes can start to oxidise the surface.
Soldering
Step 4, heat the fitting.

Light the torch and apply the flame to the body of the fitting, not to the pipe. Move the flame slowly around the fitting, heating it evenly on all sides. The flux will bubble and smoke briefly as it activates; this is normal. After 15 to 20 seconds, touch the solder wire to the joint edge. If it melts on contact and disappears into the joint, the temperature is right.
Step 5, feed the solder.

Feed the solder wire into the joint at one point on the circumference. The solder is drawn in by capillary action. Feed until a bright ring of solder appears around the full joint circumference. On a 15 mm fitting this requires approximately 10 to 12 mm of solder wire; on a 22 mm fitting approximately 15 to 18 mm. Remove the flame and allow the joint to cool undisturbed for 60 seconds.
Step 6, wipe and inspect.

Wipe the joint with a damp cloth while still warm to remove flux residue. A correctly made joint will show a bright continuous ring of solder around the full joint circumference. Any dull grey area indicates a void; reheat that area and feed additional solder while the joint is hot. Allow the joint to cool fully before restoring the water supply.
Common mistakes
Solder balling on the surface rather than entering the joint.
The fitting was not hot enough when the solder was applied. Remove the solder, reheat the fitting for a further 10 to 15 seconds, and try again.
Joint leaks when the supply is restored.

Either the joint was not fully sealed (grey area visible in the solder ring) or the solder ring was disturbed before it set. Drain the pipe, dry the joint completely, and re-solder from the beginning.
